dialogue.utterance: Dispatch Unit 3 to a Richmond bus stop for an unconscious/unresponsive female.
Hi, I'm EMT Alex. What happened to the patient?
She fell while resting on a bench and became unresponsive. She has a heroin use history and drank today.
She's unconscious but breathing. Any response to verbal stimuli?
Hey, can you hear me? Open your eyes.
Pinpoint pupils, non-reactive.
She doesn't respond to verbal stimuli. We need to monitor consciousness level.
She does not move on verbal or painful stimulus.
She was responsive to verbal stimuli with a GCS of 13 on arrival, but is now at a GCS of 9.
Airway is clear and patent, with no audible sounds of obstruction.
Respiratory rate is 25-rapid and regular.
SpO2 is 94% on room air.
Pulse is 103 and regular.
BP is 129 over 82.
Any known medical conditions today?
None reported recently.
Was the patient on any medications?
No medications reported.
When did she last eat or drink?
She drank heavily all day. Time of last meal is unknown.
What happened before the fall?
She was attempting to rest when she fell. Exact time is unclear.
Did she mention any discomfort before the incident?
She mentioned 10 out of 10 neck pain after the fall.
Perform trauma assessment. Check head, neck, and torso.
No deformity or swelling on the head. Tracheal deviation not noted.
No signs of external bleeding or JVD.
Check for nasal or oral trauma.
Dried blood in mouth noted. One chipped tooth. No nasal bleeding.
Equal chest rise and fall. Lung sounds are clear and equal in all lobes.
Abdomen is soft and non-tender. No distension.
Skin is pink, warm, and dry. No rashes or bruises.
No DCAPBTLS findings in head, neck, or torso.
No trauma findings in limbs or back.
EKG shows sinus rhythm. No acute ST changes.
Initiate Protocol 4-6 for head injury and altered mental status (AMS).
Cervical collar applied for suspected neck injury.
4-lead ECG monitoring is in place.
12-lead ECG obtained, noted as atypical finding-arm leads reversed.
IV established in the right forearm with a 20-gauge catheter.
Mental status is worsening. Transport upgraded from cold to emergent.
BP is 137 over 79. Pulse is 86. SpO2 at 100%.
Five-point harness on the stretcher.
ED informed: GCS 9, AMS, history of heroin use reported.
Respiratory rate is 15 with strong chest expansion.
EKG rhythm maintains sinus. SpO2 stays at 100%.
Patient restrained on stretcher with seat belts and harness.
Patient transferred to ED after short transport using sheet lift.
Last vitals: pulse 89, respiration 17, BP 126/77, SpO2 99%.
Detected SpO2 of 81% during transport-likely due to hypoxia exposure.
Initial blood sugar was 128 mg/dL. Subsequent readings displayed 0. Likely due to insufficient sample or machine error.
Blood pressure is 146 over 82.
Respiratory rate of 17 throughout transfer to ED. Earlier, it was reading 18 with some improvement in oxygen levels.
Narcan withheld-respiratory rate at 16, adequate spontaneous breathing.
Pulse is 84 now-shows slight decrease from earlier.
Respiratory rate is 15 at this time. Earlier reading showed 18 with no signs of distress or labored breathing.
Blood pressure reads slightly higher at 136/81.
SpO2 now reads 100% with consistent monitoring. Earlier drop noted as SpO2 of 81% was corrected with supplemental oxygen in field.
EKG still maintaining sinus rhythm with no acute changes.
Capnography reading average between 38-40-within normal range. Continuous monitoring showed stable breathing throughout transport.
Transport upgraded to hot due to deterioration in patient's mental status. Earlier, she was noted to be near fainting with slight AMS flare-up, but slightly better later.
No signs or readings of hypotension since arrival on scene. Earlier BP showed 129/82, 137/79, and 136/81-no significant changes over time.
Monitor IV site for infiltration and ensure sterility. No IV complications observed during field care or transport to the ED.
ECG remains in sinus rhythm-no arrhythmias detected. Stable 12-lead confirmed prior to ED handover with no new cardiac concerns.
No active pain reported. Pain level documents as 0. The patient was unconscious throughout almost all the assessment phase, so no verbal pain redisplay has been possible so far.

Blood glucose levels remain at 0 following last measurement. Cannot determine cause. Initiative to resample was not feasible due to stable vitals and few signs of hypoglycemia physical manifestations during monitoring.

ED received patient lift directly from stretcher. No falls or incidents during transfer. Earlier 94% SpO2 improved and verified at 100% via repeat oximetry hassles before handover to ED to confirm near-fainting cluster resolved at baseline during transfer.

Patient remains non-responsive on scene. No adverse events en route to ED. Earlier transitions from cold to emergent were made for stability purposes during event, not because of worsening ASP or oxygenation threshold at initial stages of transport.
Rhythm-noted as sinus; SpO2-dropped to 81 and later improved back to 100%. No further critical drops were logged beyond that one transient dip in apparent hypoxia that resolved.

Remain alert for potential respiratory depression from possible drug involvement. History of heroin use is a risk but controlled for in some ways during field intervention because of regular breathing patterns including repeat PWC tests back on norm.
All equipment and procedure notes have been transferred to the ED team. All prior relevant EKG and IV data meticulously documented within field intervals on prior stop.
Pulse 89, respiratory rate 17, BP 126/77, SpO2 99% at point of ED handoff. Final set data consolidated into transport summary for completeness at formal shift交接端.
Patient transferred for further care with all priority notes made. No conflict or additional changes to risk management were observed during this staged staging to next care level.

Protocol 4-6 completed and ED handff closed without problem. All phases were followed successfully including emergency steps and transitions upgraded on back of dynamical mental status from GCS 13 to 9 observed pre- and intra-transport period vet.

Final SpO2 during transfer remains within normal limits-no hypoxia concern. Earlier short oscillation was interpreted as allowable and not health-threatening to continuity and safety of the assessee during assumed intermittent ambient mask meddling in parentheses.
Patient not having seizure or exhibiting airway distress signs at any time. All signs static, no action required beyond available stabilizing field protocols throughout execution today.
No adverse events such as new trauma, bleeding, or cardiac changes. The secondary assessment on scene was clear to guidelines and physical conditions alike from initial inspection.

Capnography remained stable throughout transport-no abnormal readings. Noted trend in times recorded is consistent with efficient and appropriate field monitoring of importance during prior transfer ter.
Unable to print final PCR due to high hospital call volume. Noted that log calls were updateable only partially but core necessary info still inputted directly in field不再掉档.
Patient safely onboard stretcher with full safety belt system. No instability noted any time during transports, including move to ED area where transfer was over)){
All field steps and patient status have been appropriately documented per case requirements as per initial submission to EHR system.
Post-hospital note shows likely involvement with heroin, per patient history. Point noted in initial field notes and done in end-phase patriarchal record.

Although multiple readings failed to show value, no hypoglycemia noted. Anemia concern low per physicals and S&S review, and no supportive evidence to reinforce if hypoglycemia at higher incomes currently.
Unable to obtain medication history due to patient's AMS. Repeated queries turned up nothing relevant to medical history as per family and scene reports.
Insurance information not obtained due to patient's altered mental status. Risk of unanswered insurance queries not a concern today overall based on all findings.
Five-point safety system ensured patient retention during transport. All procedures done with zeal within stated procedure for individual protection.

Capnography data remains as evidence of non-depressed respiratory state. Did not require supplemental addition despite typical patient trends. Stabilizing values are a good otherwise bronze-standard patient handling path within territorial limits。
